sample_rows.Message:
Thanks for your answer.

This is my way : I haven't your background and I probably never have it. I must work to optimise my model instead of working features.

To do that, when it is a "true" 1 or when it's a "true" 0, the result of my kernel must be 0 or a high probabylity if it's 1. I have made some work in this direction. For example, if I delete from my train set some very bad "1". (Target is 1 but my model give a very very poor result... Like 0.1 for example..) When I supress some of this row, my model give a better result... Result for real "0" is lower and the auc is better.

I have made some submission to test... with models trained with a depleted dataset. Results are better or the same. 0.9 With this type of dataset, the real "1" have a highter value and the real "0" a lower value. Finality auc is same of better.

But I need to find cut off to do that or a rule or something... To do that I want to know if I suppress a value that exist in the test train or not...

I hope
sample_rows.RawMarkdown:
Thanks for your answer.

This is my way :
I haven't your background and I probably never have it.
I must work to optimise my model instead of working features.

To do that, when it is a "true" 1 or when it's a "true" 0, the result of my kernel must be 0 or a high probabylity if it's 1.
I have made some work in this direction.
For example, if I delete from my train set some very bad "1".
(Target is 1 but my model give a very very poor result... Like 0.1 for example..)
When I supress some of this row, my model give a better result...
Result for real "0" is lower and the auc is better.

I have made some submission to test... with models trained with a depleted dataset.
Results are better or the same. 0.9
With this type of dataset, the real "1" have a highter value and the real "0" a lower value.
Finality auc is same of better.

But I need to find cut off to do that or a rule or something...
To do that I want to know if I suppress a value that exist in the test train or not...


I hope

sample_rows.Message:
[quote=Jiahuan He;149374]

Tom is amazing. He must saw something I didn't capture. I am really looking forward to see his solution after we finish this.

[/quote]

Thanks Jiahuan, I really appreciate your kind words but I am not even sure that the difference between the current first and second(s) is statistically significant. We'll know soon enough!
